channel.title: Org Management on Multi-Agent Future
channel.description: Real stories of building AI multi-agent systems — from automated trading bots to AI-managed organizations. No hype, just code and lessons.

item.title: How AI Is Flattening the Org Chart and Rewriting Middle Management
item.description:
The Prediction That Got Everyone’s Attention

In October 2024, Gartner dropped a prediction that traveled through every Slack channel and LinkedIn feed in corporate America: “Through 2026, 20% of organizations will use AI to flatten their organizational structure, eliminating more than half of current middle management positions.”

We’re now in March 2026. So where do we actually stand?

The short answer: the prediction wasn’t wrong, but the execution has been far messier than the clean narrative suggests. Some companies have aggressively cut middle management and seen real gains. Others did the same and are now quietly rehiring. The pattern is more interesting — and more instructive — than the headline.
item.content:encoded:
The Prediction That Got Everyone’s Attention

In October 2024, Gartner dropped a prediction that traveled through every Slack channel and LinkedIn feed in corporate America: “Through 2026, 20% of organizations will use AI to flatten their organizational structure, eliminating more than half of current middle management positions.”

We’re now in March 2026. So where do we actually stand?

The short answer: the prediction wasn’t wrong, but the execution has been far messier than the clean narrative suggests. Some companies have aggressively cut middle management and seen real gains. Others did the same and are now quietly rehiring. The pattern is more interesting — and more instructive — than the headline.

This post breaks down what’s actually happening on the ground, which roles are genuinely at risk, what the early movers got right and wrong, and what a realistic AI-augmented org structure looks like for companies that want to move fast without breaking their culture.

What Middle Managers Actually Do (And Which Parts AI Can Replace)

Before we talk about replacing middle management, we need to be honest about what they do. The caricature — the pointy-haired boss from Dilbert who adds no value — obscures a more complex reality.

Middle managers typically handle five categories of work:

1. Information routing and status reporting Collecting updates from teams, synthesizing them into dashboards or reports, and passing information up the chain. This is the most automatable function. AI agents already do this better than humans — they don’t forget, they don’t editorialize, and they work 24/7.

2. Task allocation and scheduling Deciding who works on what, managing calendars, resolving resource conflicts. Project management tools with AI capabilities (Asana’s AI features, Monday.com’s automations, Linear’s auto-assignment) have been chipping away at this for years. Agentic AI is finishing the job.

3. Performance monitoring Tracking KPIs, identifying underperformers, flagging risks. This is where AI genuinely excels — pattern recognition across large datasets, anomaly detection, and trend analysis. McKinsey’s 2025 State of AI survey found that redesigning workflows around AI had the single biggest impact on organizational performance across all 25 attributes they tested.

4. Decision-making within defined parameters Approving budgets under a threshold, deciding on routine escalations, choosing between predefined options. Rule-based decisions are exactly what AI handles well, especially when the decision criteria are documented.

5. People development, coaching, and culture Mentoring, resolving interpersonal conflicts, building team cohesion, navigating ambiguity, advocating for reports. This is where AI falls flat — and where the companies that over-automated are now paying the price.

The companies getting this right are automating categories 1-4 and reinvesting in category 5. The ones getting it wrong are treating all five categories as equivalent.

The Klarna Experiment: A Cautionary Tale in Three Acts

No company has been more publicly aggressive about AI-driven workforce reduction than Klarna, the Swedish buy-now-pay-later company. Their story is worth examining in detail because it contains every lesson about AI organizational transformation compressed into 18 months.

Act 1: The Bold Move (2024)

Klarna CEO Sebastian Siemiatkowski announced that the company’s AI assistant, powered by OpenAI, was doing the work of 700 full-time customer service agents. The company instituted a hiring freeze and let attrition do its work. By mid-2025, Klarna had shrunk from roughly 5,527 employees to around 2,907 — a 40% reduction. The AI was handling two-thirds of all customer service conversations.

The financial results looked spectacular. Revenue per employee skyrocketed. Marketing spend dropped. Siemiatkowski told the press that AI was handling the work of 853 full-time staff and the company was raising remaining employee salaries by 60%.

Act 2: The Cracks Appear (Late 2025)

Then the metrics that weren’t in the press releases started surfacing. Customer complaints increased. Satisfaction ratings dropped. The AI handled volume well but struggled with nuanced, complex, or emotionally charged interactions — exactly the ones that determine whether a customer stays or leaves. Reports emerged of generic, repetitive, and insufficiently nuanced automated responses.

The problem wasn’t the AI itself. The problem was that Klarna had removed the human judgment layer that knew when to escalate, how to read between the lines of a customer complaint, and when a policy exception would save a relationship worth more than the cost of the exception.

Act 3: The Reversal (2026)

Siemiatkowski eventually acknowledged the cuts had gone too far. Klarna started hiring again, specifically for roles that required human judgment and relationship management. A 2025 Orgvue survey found that 55% of companies that made employees redundant due to AI now regret those decisions.

The lesson isn’t that AI doesn’t work for organizational restructuring. It’s that treating AI deployment as a headcount reduction exercise instead of a role redesign exercise produces predictable failures.

Who’s Getting Organizational AI Right

Not every company followed the Klarna playbook. Some have been more thoughtful, and their results are more sustainable.

Salesforce: The “Digital Workforce” Model

Salesforce’s approach with Agentforce is instructive. Rather than replacing roles, they added a layer of AI agents that handle specific workflow segments — simulating product launches, orchestrating marketing campaigns, handling first-line customer queries. CEO Marc Benioff described it as creating a “digital workforce” where humans and agents work together.

The key distinction: Salesforce didn’t eliminate the product launch manager. They gave the product launch manager an AI team that handles the data collection, scheduling, stakeholder tracking, and status reporting so the human can focus on strategy, relationships, and the judgment calls that determine whether the launch succeeds.

What “AI High Performers” Do Differently

McKinsey’s data is revealing here. Their 2025 survey of nearly 2,000 organizations found that high-performing companies consistently differ from the rest across six dimensions: strategy, talent, operating model, technology, data, and adoption.

The critical finding: 32% of companies expect AI to reduce their total workforce by at least 3% within the next year, but AI high performers are more likely to predict both reductions and increases — they’re restructuring, not just cutting. They’re eliminating roles that are primarily information-routing while creating new roles focused on AI oversight, prompt engineering, and cross-functional coordination.

The companies that just cut headcount and declare victory are the ones that end up in the Orgvue regret statistics.

The New Org Chart: What Flatter Actually Looks Like

When Gartner talks about “flattening” organizational structure, what does that mean in practice? Based on early adopters, the pattern looks like this:

Before: The Traditional Pyramid

CEO
  └─ VP (Strategy, Budget)
       └─ Director (Department Goals, Resource Allocation)
            └─ Senior Manager (Team Oversight, Reporting)
                 └─ Manager (Task Assignment, Performance Tracking)
                      └─ Team Lead (Daily Coordination)
                           └─ Individual Contributors

Six layers between the person doing the work and the person setting the strategy.

After: The AI-Augmented Diamond

CEO + AI Strategy Dashboard
  └─ VP (Strategy + AI-Surfaced Insights)
       └─ Team Lead / Player-Coach (People + AI Agent Oversight)
            └─ Individual Contributors + AI Agents

Three to four layers. The Director, Senior Manager, and Manager layers collapse because:

  • Information routing → handled by AI dashboards and automated reports
  • Task allocation → handled by AI project management agents
  • Performance monitoring → handled by AI analytics with exception-based human review
  • Routine decisions → handled by AI within defined policy parameters
  • People development → stays human, elevated in importance

The remaining human manager role transforms into what some organizations call a “player-coach” — someone who both contributes individual work and handles the irreducibly human aspects of team leadership.

Duolingo: The AI-First Rewrite

Duolingo provides another data point. In January 2024, the language-learning company cut 10% of its contractor workforce, primarily content writers and translators, replacing them with AI. By mid-2025, CEO Luis von Ahn announced the company would phase out contractors entirely for work AI could handle, declaring Duolingo an “AI-first” company.

The organizational impact went beyond headcount. Duolingo’s content creation pipeline — which previously required human translators coordinated by project managers coordinated by content directors — collapsed into a system where a smaller team of AI trainers and quality reviewers worked directly with LLM-based generation tools.

The middle management layer that existed to coordinate human translators across 40+ languages became unnecessary when the coordination target shifted from humans to AI models.

This is the pattern that Gartner is tracking: it’s not that AI replaces the manager directly. It’s that AI replaces the work that required the manager to exist.

The Skills That Survive Organizational Flattening

If you’re a middle manager reading this and feeling uneasy, the productive question isn’t “will my job be eliminated?” It’s “which parts of my current role will still need a human in 18 months?”

Based on where early adopters are landing, these skills are appreciating in value:

1. Ambiguity Navigation

AI is excellent at optimizing within defined parameters. It’s terrible at deciding which parameters matter. When a project faces competing priorities with no clear framework for resolution — a client wants faster delivery but the team is burning out, or a cost cut will save money this quarter but damage a strategic relationship — that’s a human judgment call that requires understanding context, relationships, politics, and values simultaneously.

2. Cross-Functional Translation

As organizations flatten, the remaining human managers need to translate between domains more than ever. The engineering team, the sales team, and the finance team all have their own languages, priorities, and definitions of success. AI can surface data across these domains, but interpreting what that data means in context — and negotiating trade-offs that all parties can live with — requires human social intelligence.

3. Trust Building and Conflict Resolution

You cannot automate trust. When a team member is struggling, when two colleagues have a conflict that’s affecting output, when someone needs to hear hard feedback delivered with empathy — these moments define whether a team functions or fractures. Companies that eliminated these roles are learning that the cost of un-managed teams shows up in attrition, and attrition is expensive.

4. AI Oversight and Prompt Architecture

This is the genuinely new skill. Someone needs to design the prompts and workflows that AI agents follow, monitor their outputs for quality and bias, and know when to override automated decisions. This isn’t a junior role — it requires deep domain knowledge plus AI literacy. The best candidates are often experienced middle managers who understand the business context deeply enough to know when the AI is wrong.

5. Strategic Narrative and Change Communication

When organizations restructure — especially AI-driven restructuring that triggers job security anxiety — someone needs to explain why, paint a credible picture of the future, and maintain morale through the transition. This is leadership work that AI cannot do, and it’s the work that separates successful transformations from the 55% that end in regret.

A Practical Framework: The 70/20/10 Restructuring Model

For organizations considering AI-driven org flattening, here’s a framework based on what the successful early adopters are doing:

70% Augmentation

Take 70% of middle management tasks and augment them with AI. Don’t eliminate the role — transform it. The manager who spent 4 hours per day on status reports and task tracking now spends 30 minutes reviewing AI-generated reports and 3.5 hours on coaching, strategy, and cross-functional work.

20% Automation

Identify the 20% of roles that are genuinely and primarily information-routing. These are the roles where the person’s main contribution is collecting data from one group and presenting it to another. Automate these, but do it with a transition plan — retraining, redeployment, or generous severance.

10% Creation

Create new roles that didn’t exist before: AI operations managers, prompt engineers, human-AI workflow designers, AI ethics and quality reviewers. Hire from your existing middle management pool when possible — they have the domain knowledge that makes AI oversight effective.

Audit Prompt for Your Organization:

List every middle management role. For each one, categorize the 
time split across these five functions:

1. Information routing/reporting: ___% 
2. Task allocation/scheduling: ___%
3. Performance monitoring: ___%
4. Routine decision-making: ___%
5. People development/coaching/culture: ___%

Roles where #1-4 total > 80%: candidates for automation
Roles where #5 > 30%: candidates for augmentation and elevation
Roles where #1-4 total > 80% AND #5 < 10%: candidates for elimination

Run this audit before making any headcount decisions.

The Gartner Prediction, Revisited

So is Gartner’s prediction coming true? In March 2026, the answer is “partially, and not in the way most people expected.”

Yes, organizations are flattening. The McKinsey data confirms that workflow redesign is the single most impactful organizational change companies can make around AI. The IMD forecasts for 2026 describe this year as the shift from scaling AI pilots to full organizational overhaul.

But the 20% of organizations doing this successfully aren’t eliminating middle management wholesale. They’re decomposing the role into its component functions, automating the mechanical parts, and elevating the human parts. The organizations that treated this as a cost-cutting exercise are the ones now dealing with regret, rehiring, and the morale damage that comes from employees watching their colleagues get replaced by chatbots.

The real prediction should have been: “Through 2026, 20% of organizations will fundamentally redesign middle management roles around AI capabilities, and the ones that confuse redesign with elimination will learn an expensive lesson about what humans actually do.”

What to Do This Quarter

Whether you’re a CEO planning a restructure or a middle manager planning your career, here are concrete next steps:

If you lead an organization:

  • Run the audit prompt above before making any structural changes
  • Start with augmentation pilots, not elimination programs
  • Measure what happens to team satisfaction and retention when you automate management tasks — not just what happens to cost
  • Talk to Klarna’s competitors about what happened to their customer satisfaction during the aggressive-cut phase

If you’re a middle manager:

  • Audit your own time. Honestly categorize where your hours go across the five functions above
  • If more than 70% of your time is information routing and task tracking, you’re in the automation zone — start shifting toward coaching, strategy, and cross-functional work now
  • Learn AI tools deeply enough to oversee them, not just use them
  • Position yourself as the person who knows the domain well enough to tell the AI when it’s wrong

If you’re building AI agent systems:

  • Design for augmentation first, replacement second
  • Build in human escalation paths that are easy to use, not friction-heavy “are you sure?” dialogues
  • Instrument your systems to measure quality degradation, not just volume metrics
  • Remember that the Klarna lesson applies to your customers too

What’s Next in This Series

Next week, we’ll look at how AI is reshaping performance reviews and feedback cycles — another middle management staple that’s being fundamentally rethought. We’ll examine companies that have replaced annual reviews with AI-powered continuous feedback systems and whether the data supports the hype.

This post is part of the AI & Organization Management series, exploring how artificial intelligence is transforming organizational structure, leadership, and team dynamics with real case studies and actionable frameworks.

item.title: AI Is Killing the Annual Performance Review (Finally)
item.description:
The Annual Review Is a Ritual Everyone Hates

Here’s a number that should embarrass every HR department in America: 90% of managers are dissatisfied with their own annual review process. Not 90% of employees — 90% of the people running the reviews.

The annual performance review is one of those corporate traditions that persists not because it works, but because nobody has found a replacement that satisfies legal, HR compliance teams, and executive dashboards simultaneously. It’s the organizational equivalent of the fax machine — everyone knows it’s broken, most workplaces still have one.
item.content:encoded:
The Annual Review Is a Ritual Everyone Hates

Here’s a number that should embarrass every HR department in America: 90% of managers are dissatisfied with their own annual review process. Not 90% of employees — 90% of the people running the reviews.

The annual performance review is one of those corporate traditions that persists not because it works, but because nobody has found a replacement that satisfies legal, HR compliance teams, and executive dashboards simultaneously. It’s the organizational equivalent of the fax machine — everyone knows it’s broken, most workplaces still have one.

The evidence has been piling up for a decade. One-third of U.S. companies have been actively moving away from traditional appraisals since 2016. Deloitte, Accenture, Adobe, and GE all publicly ditched their annual review processes. But for the remaining two-thirds, inertia won.

Until now. AI-powered performance management tools are doing something the previous wave of “continuous feedback” platforms never quite pulled off: they’re making the alternative less work, not more. And that changes the calculus entirely.

Why Previous Attempts to Kill the Annual Review Failed

Before we get to the AI angle, it’s worth understanding why the “death of the annual review” has been predicted roughly once per year since 2014, and why it never quite stuck.

The problem wasn’t the diagnosis. Everyone agreed that a single annual conversation was insufficient to drive performance. The problem was the prescription.

Continuous feedback requires continuous effort. When companies like Adobe replaced annual reviews with “Check-Ins,” they were asking managers to have frequent, structured conversations with each direct report. In theory, great. In practice, you’re asking the busiest people in the organization to do more of the thing they already don’t have time for.

Weekly check-in tools created notification fatigue. Platforms like 15Five built their entire product around weekly manager-employee check-ins. The early adoption curves looked promising. The 12-month retention curves did not. Managers started treating the weekly prompts the same way most of us treat the “how was your experience?” emails from airlines — ignored until absolutely forced.

HR lost its audit trail. Annual reviews, for all their flaws, produce a document. That document is discoverable in lawsuits, useful for promotion justification, and required by many compliance frameworks. When you replace one annual document with 52 weekly conversations, the question becomes: who’s writing all that down? The answer, usually, was nobody.

Calibration became impossible. One of the hidden functions of annual review cycles is calibration — the process where managers compare ratings across teams to ensure consistency. Hard to calibrate when Team A does monthly check-ins, Team B does quarterly reviews, and Team C’s manager forgot to log anything since August.

So the annual review persisted, not because anyone loved it, but because the alternatives created new problems without fully solving the old ones.

What’s Different About AI-Powered Performance Management

The current generation of AI performance tools isn’t just adding a chatbot to the old process. It’s restructuring how performance data gets captured, synthesized, and acted on. The shift is architectural, not cosmetic.

Passive Data Collection Replaces Active Documentation

The fundamental breakthrough is this: instead of asking humans to document performance continuously, AI systems observe work artifacts that already exist.

Windmill, one of the more aggressive players in this space, integrates with Slack, GitHub, Jira, and calendar tools to build a continuous picture of what each employee is actually doing. When review time comes, the system drafts a summary based on months of observed activity — commits merged, projects shipped, meetings led, peer interactions logged.

Culture Amp takes a different approach, connecting employee engagement survey data to performance patterns. Their AI identifies correlations that human managers miss: the team whose engagement scores dropped three months before their delivery metrics declined, or the individual whose peer recognition frequency predicts their likelihood of promotion.

The critical distinction from previous continuous feedback tools: the AI does the documentation work. Managers review and edit a draft rather than starting from a blank page. According to Lattice, managers currently spend one to two weeks completing a single review cycle. AI-assisted reviews cut that to hours.

Continuous Signal, Periodic Synthesis

The smarter implementations have landed on a hybrid model that I think will become standard: AI captures signals continuously, but humans still make decisions at defined intervals.

This matters because it addresses the HR audit trail problem. You get a documented review at whatever cadence your legal team requires — quarterly, semi-annually, annually — but that document is built from months of continuous observation rather than two weeks of panicked recollection.

Betterworks and Leapsome both use this approach. Their AI systems generate what amounts to a “first draft” performance review, pre-populated with specific examples, goal progress data, and peer feedback summaries. The manager’s job becomes editorial: confirm, adjust, add context, have the conversation.

Calibration at Scale

This might be the most underappreciated capability. AI can compare language, rating patterns, and evaluation criteria across hundreds of reviews simultaneously — something that calibration committees attempt to do in a conference room over three hours with rapidly declining attention spans.

PerformYard specifically markets bias detection in review language. Their system flags reviews that use different adjective patterns for different demographic groups — a known problem in manual reviews where, for example, women are more likely to receive feedback about communication style while men receive feedback about technical outcomes.

The Lattice Cautionary Tale

No discussion of AI in performance management is complete without the Lattice story, because it illustrates exactly where the boundary sits between helpful augmentation and organizational overcorrection.

In July 2024, Lattice announced they would be the first platform to give AI “digital workers” official employee records — onboarding them, training them, assigning goals, performance metrics, and even managers. Just like human employees.

The backlash was immediate and fierce. HR professionals and tech workers pushed back hard. Within days, Lattice reversed course, posting that they “will not further pursue digital workers in the product.”

Then, in March 2025, Lattice CEO Sarah Franklin told Forbes that AI agents still need to be rated on their performance — just as human workers are. The framing had evolved from “AI as employee” to “AI as tool that needs evaluation,” but the underlying tension remained.

The lesson here is crucial for any organization implementing AI in performance management: the technology works best when it augments human judgment, not when it replaces human identity. Employees don’t mind AI helping their manager write a better review. They very much mind being treated as equivalent to a chatbot.

The Legal Minefield Nobody’s Talking About

If you’re implementing AI-assisted performance reviews, your legal team needs to be in the room. Here’s why.

Automated Decision Systems and Employment Law

The HR Defense Blog reported that multiple jurisdictions now classify AI performance tools as “automated-decision systems” (ADS), making it unlawful to use any ADS that discriminates against employees based on protected traits in personnel decisions.

New York City’s Local Law 144 already requires annual bias audits for automated employment decision tools. Illinois, Colorado, and the EU (under the AI Act) have similar or stricter requirements taking effect in 2025 and 2026.

The practical implication: if your AI performance tool surfaces a recommendation — “this employee is underperforming based on our analysis” — and that recommendation disproportionately affects a protected class, your company is exposed. CPO Magazine’s 2026 legal forecast warns that using AI algorithms without bias audits can lead to class-action exposure under Title VII and ADEA.

The Bias Problem Is Real, Not Hypothetical

A 2025 University of Melbourne study found that AI-powered hiring tools struggled to accurately evaluate candidates with speech disabilities or heavy non-native accents. The same pattern applies to performance evaluation: if your AI is analyzing Slack messages, it will evaluate differently based on writing style, English proficiency, and communication frequency — all of which correlate with protected characteristics.

This doesn’t mean you shouldn’t use AI in performance management. It means you need:

  1. Regular bias audits by an independent third party
  2. Transparency about what data the AI uses and how
  3. Human override on every consequential decision (termination, promotion, compensation)
  4. Documentation that the AI’s role is advisory, not determinative

How to Actually Implement This (Without Getting Fired or Sued)

Here’s the practical playbook, based on what I’ve seen working at companies that are doing this well.

Phase 1: Instrument Before You Automate (Weeks 1-4)

Before you buy any AI performance tool, you need to understand what performance data you already generate.

Audit your existing tool stack. Map every system where work artifacts live: project management (Jira, Asana, Linear), communication (Slack, Teams), code (GitHub, GitLab), documents (Google Workspace, Notion), CRM (Salesforce, HubSpot). This becomes your AI’s observation surface.

Identify your review cadence requirements. Talk to legal and compliance. What documentation do they need, and how often? This determines whether you’re building a quarterly, semi-annual, or annual synthesis cycle.

Benchmark your current process cost. Measure how many hours managers spend on reviews today. You’ll need this number to justify the investment and to measure whether the AI actually reduced the burden.

EXECUTION PROMPT — Tool Stack Audit

"List every tool where our team creates work artifacts (code commits, 
project updates, messages, documents, meeting notes). For each tool, 
identify: (1) Does it have an API? (2) What performance-relevant data 
does it contain? (3) Who owns the admin access? Create a spreadsheet 
with columns: Tool | Data Type | API Available | Admin Owner | 
Integration Priority (High/Medium/Low)"

Phase 2: Pick Your Platform (Weeks 4-6)

The market is crowded. Here’s how the major players differentiate:

Platform Best For AI Capability Price Range
Windmill Engineering teams Slack-first, auto-drafts from work data Mid-range
Culture Amp Enterprise, engagement-focused Connects surveys to performance Premium
Lattice General purpose, mid-market Goal tracking + AI review drafts Mid-range
Leapsome European companies (GDPR) Multi-source AI synthesis Mid-range
Betterworks Large enterprise OKR alignment + AI calibration Premium
PerformYard Bias-conscious orgs Language analysis + demographic flagging Budget-friendly

My honest recommendation: if you’re under 200 employees, start with Leapsome or PerformYard. If you’re engineering-heavy, look at Windmill. If you’re enterprise with an existing Culture Amp engagement deployment, extend it into performance. Don’t buy a platform just because it has the flashiest AI demo.

Phase 3: Run a Parallel Cycle (Months 2-4)

Do not — I repeat, do not — replace your existing review process on day one. Run the AI system in parallel for at least one full review cycle.

This means managers complete their normal reviews AND receive the AI-generated draft. Then you compare:

  • Accuracy: Did the AI capture the same themes the manager identified?
  • Completeness: Did the AI surface things the manager missed (or vice versa)?
  • Bias: Did the AI’s language patterns differ across demographic groups?
  • Time savings: How long did the AI-assisted version take vs. the traditional version?
EXECUTION PROMPT — Parallel Review Analysis

"Compare the AI-generated review draft with the manager-written review 
for each employee. Score on: (1) Theme alignment (1-5), (2) Specific 
example quality (1-5), (3) Actionable feedback presence (Y/N), 
(4) Any concerning language patterns. Summarize findings across all 
reviews in an executive brief with a go/no-go recommendation for 
full deployment."

Phase 4: Transition With Manager Training (Months 4-6)

The biggest failure mode isn’t the technology. It’s managers who either rubber-stamp the AI output without adding judgment, or managers who ignore the AI output entirely and revert to their old habits.

You need structured training that covers:

  1. How to edit an AI draft — where to add personal context, specific examples, and forward-looking development goals that the AI can’t observe
  2. When to override — recognizing when the AI’s data picture is incomplete (parental leave, internal transfer, project scope change)
  3. Legal guardrails — what language to avoid, how to document that the final review reflects human judgment
  4. The conversation itself — AI can write the document, but the human delivers it face-to-face

Phase 5: Measure and Iterate (Ongoing)

Track three metrics quarterly:

  1. Manager time per review cycle — this should drop 40-60% in the first year
  2. Employee satisfaction with feedback quality — survey this; it should improve
  3. Bias audit results — run these quarterly with the vendor or an independent firm

If manager time isn’t dropping, the tool isn’t integrated properly. If employee satisfaction isn’t improving, the managers are either rubber-stamping or the AI’s quality is poor. If bias audits show problems, pause and fix before continuing.

What This Looks Like in Two Years

Here’s my prediction for where AI performance management lands by 2028:

The annual review becomes a quarterly synthesis. The annual cadence dies not with a dramatic announcement but with a gradual compression. Companies move to quarterly AI-generated summaries with annual compensation decisions. The “review” becomes a conversation about a document that largely wrote itself.

Manager quality becomes more visible, not less. When the AI handles documentation, what’s left for the manager is pure leadership: coaching, context-setting, career development, difficult conversations. The managers who were hiding behind the paperwork get exposed. The managers who were good at the human parts get more time to do them.

Performance data becomes bidirectional. Current tools evaluate employees. Next-generation tools will evaluate managers too — analyzing how their feedback patterns, response times, and coaching quality correlate with team outcomes. Gallup’s data showing that only 21% of employees feel they control their own performance metrics will start to shift as AI makes the evaluation process more transparent and data-driven.

Regulatory frameworks catch up. The patchwork of NYC, Illinois, Colorado, and EU regulations will consolidate into something more coherent. Companies that got ahead of bias audits and transparency requirements will have a significant advantage over those that didn’t.

The Bottom Line

The annual performance review has survived for decades not because it works, but because the alternatives were more work for everyone involved. AI changes that equation by handling the documentation burden — the part humans are worst at — while leaving the judgment, coaching, and human connection to the people who should be doing it.

58% of companies still track performance in spreadsheets. If that’s you, you’re not just behind on technology. You’re building legal exposure, burning manager time, and producing reviews that neither managers nor employees trust.

The path forward isn’t replacing human judgment with AI. It’s freeing human judgment from busywork so it can actually matter.

This is Part 2 of the AI & Organization Management series. Part 1 covered how AI is flattening the org chart and rewriting middle management. Next up: AI-powered hiring — what actually works, what’s hype, and the legal framework you need before you deploy any of it.

item.title: AI Is Gutting HR Departments — Here's What Actually Replaces Them
item.description:
IBM Just Showed Everyone the Endgame

In May 2025, IBM made an announcement that should have been front-page news in every business publication. Their internal AI agent, AskHR, now handles 94% of routine HR questions across the entire company. Not 94% attempted — 94% resolved without human intervention. The system generated over 11.5 million employee interactions in 2024 alone and reduced support tickets by 75% since its initial deployment.

The immediate consequence: IBM’s CEO confirmed plans to shift HR headcount budget toward sales and engineering. The HR Business Partner role — the generalist who sits between employees and the HR function — has been effectively eliminated except for senior leadership support.
item.content:encoded:
IBM Just Showed Everyone the Endgame

In May 2025, IBM made an announcement that should have been front-page news in every business publication. Their internal AI agent, AskHR, now handles 94% of routine HR questions across the entire company. Not 94% attempted — 94% resolved without human intervention. The system generated over 11.5 million employee interactions in 2024 alone and reduced support tickets by 75% since its initial deployment.

The immediate consequence: IBM’s CEO confirmed plans to shift HR headcount budget toward sales and engineering. The HR Business Partner role — the generalist who sits between employees and the HR function — has been effectively eliminated except for senior leadership support.

This isn’t a pilot program at a scrappy startup. This is a 280,000-person enterprise that spent nearly a decade iterating on AI-powered HR, starting with CogniPay in 2018 for AI-driven compensation recommendations and expanding outward from there. IBM’s CHRO Nickle Lamoureux told analyst Josh Bersin that the AI agent now writes performance reviews, creates development plans, and coaches managers through complex performance decisions.

Bersin’s response was blunt: he expects AI to drive a 20-30% reduction in HR headcount per employee across the industry. Not at some distant future date. Now. Within current budget cycles.

If you work in HR, manage an HR team, or depend on HR services as a line manager, the ground beneath you shifted last year. This post maps the new terrain.

The Four HR Functions Getting Automated First

Not all of HR is equally exposed. The functions getting replaced fastest share common characteristics: they’re high-volume, rule-based, and historically dependent on humans acting as intermediaries between employees and information systems.

1. Employee Service Desk and Benefits Administration

This is where IBM started, and it’s the lowest-hanging fruit for every company. The majority of HR interactions are transactional: How do I enroll in dental insurance? What’s the PTO policy for bereavement? How do I update my direct deposit?

These questions follow predictable patterns. An AI agent trained on company policy documents can answer them with higher accuracy and lower latency than a human generalist who has to look up the same documents. IBM’s 94% containment rate proves the concept at scale.

The tools enabling this aren’t exotic. ServiceNow’s HR Service Delivery platform, Workday’s assistant features, and a growing crop of startups like Leena AI and Espressive are all competing in this space. Most enterprise HR tech vendors now ship some version of an AI-powered service desk.

The displacement is real. Companies running these tools typically reduce their HR service center headcount by 40-60% within the first two years. The remaining staff handles escalations, edge cases, and the kind of emotionally complex situations (harassment complaints, accommodation requests, termination logistics) where AI still falls short.

2. Resume Screening and Initial Candidate Filtering

The volume problem in recruiting is staggering. A single job posting on LinkedIn can generate hundreds or thousands of applications. Before AI tools, recruiters spent roughly 23 hours screening resumes for a single hire. Most of that time was spent on candidates who were clearly unqualified.

AI screening tools from vendors like HireVue, Pymetrics (now part of Harver), and Eightfold AI now handle initial filtering at scale. They parse resumes, match skills to job requirements, and produce ranked candidate lists. Some incorporate video interview analysis, scoring candidates on communication patterns and competency signals.

The efficiency gain is undeniable. But here’s where the story gets complicated — and where the courtroom enters the picture.

3. Compensation Analysis and Pay Equity

IBM’s CogniPay was ahead of its time in 2018, using AI to generate pay recommendations based on skill assessments rather than job titles alone. The broader market has caught up. Tools like Syndio, Payscale, and Carta Total Comp now offer AI-driven compensation benchmarking that can flag pay equity issues, recommend adjustments, and model the cost impact of proposed changes in real time.

The old model — where a comp analyst spent weeks building spreadsheets from salary survey data — is being compressed into minutes. More importantly, AI tools can continuously monitor for pay gaps across gender, race, and other protected categories, something that annual audits fundamentally couldn’t do.

This is one area where AI in HR genuinely improves outcomes rather than just cutting costs. Pay equity lawsuits cost companies an average of $2.5 million to settle, and continuous monitoring is cheaper than periodic audits that discover problems after they’ve compounded for years.

4. Learning and Development Recommendations

The corporate training industry has been ripe for disruption for decades. Most L&D programs operate on the “catalog” model — here’s a list of courses, pick what interests you. Completion rates are abysmal, relevance is questionable, and the connection between training and actual performance improvement is tenuous at best.

AI-powered platforms like Degreed, EdCast (now part of Cornerstone), and LinkedIn Learning’s recommendation engine are shifting toward personalized development paths. They analyze an employee’s current skills, their role requirements, their career trajectory, and the company’s strategic priorities, then recommend specific learning sequences.

The more sophisticated implementations — like what WPP built using OpenAI and Reejig — go further. WPP used AI to rationalize 65,000 job titles down to 600 and then mapped skills gaps across the entire organization, giving L&D teams actual data about where to invest training budgets rather than relying on manager gut feelings.

The Bias Problem Nobody Solved

Here’s where the optimistic narrative about AI in HR runs into a wall. The same tools that screen resumes faster and more consistently than humans also encode and amplify bias in ways that are proving genuinely dangerous.

The Workday Lawsuit

In one of the most significant AI employment cases working through U.S. courts, Derek Mobley filed suit against Workday alleging that its AI-powered screening tools discriminated against him based on race, age, and disability. Mobley, who is Black and over 40, applied to more than 100 jobs through employers using Workday’s platform and was rejected by all of them.

The case reached a critical milestone in May 2025 when the court granted conditional certification as a collective action under the Age Discrimination in Employment Act (ADEA). The class now includes all applicants aged 40 and over who applied through Workday’s platform from September 2020 onward and were denied.

This matters for two reasons. First, it establishes that AI vendors — not just the employers using their tools — can be held liable for discriminatory outcomes. Second, the collective action certification means the financial exposure for Workday could be enormous.

Workday has denied all claims and insists its tools don’t make hiring decisions. But the court’s willingness to let the case proceed signals that “the algorithm did it” isn’t going to work as a defense.

The ACLU vs. Aon

In a separate action, the ACLU challenged three of Aon’s AI hiring assessment tools — ADEPT-15, vidAssess-AI, and gridChallenge — alleging they discriminate against people with disabilities and certain racial groups. The complaint also targeted Aon’s marketing, which promoted the tools as “bias-free.”

The vidAssess-AI tool is particularly controversial. It analyzes video interviews for behavioral signals, a methodology that disability rights advocates argue inherently disadvantages candidates with conditions affecting speech patterns, facial expressions, or motor control.

Research Confirms the Pattern

The lawsuits aren’t outliers. Research published through VoxDev in May 2025 found that AI hiring tools systematically favored female applicants over Black male applicants with identical qualifications. The bias wasn’t random — it was consistent and directional, suggesting the training data itself encoded historical discrimination patterns.

This creates a genuine dilemma for HR leaders. The efficiency gains from AI screening are real and significant. But deploying tools that produce discriminatory outcomes exposes the company to litigation, reputational damage, and regulatory action — especially as states like Illinois, New York City, and Colorado have enacted or proposed AI hiring transparency laws.

The Entry-Level Pipeline Crisis

Korn Ferry’s 2026 Talent Acquisition Trends report identified what might be the most consequential second-order effect of AI in HR: the gutting of entry-level positions.

The numbers are stark. 43% of companies plan to replace roles with AI, with operations/back-office staff (58%) and entry-level positions (37%) as the top targets.

The boardroom logic is straightforward. Entry-level employees are expensive relative to their immediate output. They require training, supervision, and time to become productive. An AI agent that handles the same tasks costs a fraction of the salary with no onboarding period.

But this logic ignores where future leaders come from. Most managers and directors didn’t parachute into their roles. They started by filing papers, running reports, coordinating meetings, and doing the routine work that taught them how the business actually operates. Cut those entry-level roles, and in three to five years you face a leadership pipeline that’s empty.

Korn Ferry puts it directly: “Your cost saving today is rapidly becoming your talent crisis tomorrow.”

The companies getting this right are those that redesign entry-level roles rather than eliminate them. Instead of hiring a coordinator to schedule meetings and compile reports (tasks AI handles trivially), they hire an analyst who works alongside AI tools, learns to evaluate their output, and develops the judgment that will make them an effective manager in five years.

What “AI-First HR” Actually Looks Like

If the old model was a large HR department processing transactions and answering questions, the new model is a smaller team doing fundamentally different work. Here’s what the emerging structure looks like at companies that have made the transition thoughtfully.

The Shrinking Core

A mid-size company (1,000-5,000 employees) that previously maintained an HR team of 30-50 people is likely to stabilize at 15-25 within the next two years if they deploy AI tools aggressively. The reductions come almost entirely from:

  • Service desk and benefits administration (replaced by AI agents)
  • Recruiting coordination (replaced by ATS automation and AI screening)
  • Comp analysis and reporting (replaced by continuous monitoring tools)
  • Basic L&D administration (replaced by personalized learning platforms)

The Roles That Grow

While headcount shrinks, certain roles become more important and more senior:

AI Operations Manager (HR): Someone needs to train, monitor, and audit the AI tools. This role didn’t exist three years ago. It requires a combination of HR domain knowledge and technical literacy — understanding what the models are doing well enough to catch when they’re producing biased or incorrect outputs.

Employee Experience Designer: With transactions automated, the remaining human touchpoints become disproportionately important. The onboarding experience, the manager coaching session, the career development conversation — these moments now carry more weight because they’re among the few times an employee interacts with an actual human in HR.

Change Management Specialist: IBM didn’t automate its HR function overnight. It took nearly a decade of iterating, training, and managing employee expectations. Every company going through this transition needs people who can manage the human side of the change — and ironically, this is one of the things AI still does poorly.

Org Design Strategist: With AI tools providing real-time data on skills gaps, performance patterns, and workforce composition, companies can redesign their organizations more frequently and with better data. The strategist who can interpret this data and translate it into structural decisions becomes essential.

The Critical Thinking Gap

Korn Ferry’s survey revealed a striking disconnect. While executives and boards rank AI skills as the most-wanted capability for new hires, 73% of talent acquisition leaders rank critical thinking as their number-one recruiting priority. AI skills ranked fifth.

The TA leaders are right. You can teach someone to use ChatGPT in a week. Teaching them to evaluate whether its output makes sense, spot the errors, and know when human judgment should override machine logic — that takes years of practice and a fundamentally different cognitive skill set.

The companies hiring for AI proficiency are solving yesterday’s problem. The tools will keep getting easier to use. The companies hiring for critical thinking are building teams that can adapt to whatever the tools become next.

The Hybrid Workforce Nobody Planned For

Korn Ferry’s most provocative finding: more than half of talent leaders are planning to add autonomous AI agents to their teams in 2026 — not as tools, but as something closer to digital teammates with their own identities, permissions, and responsibilities.

This raises questions that HR frameworks aren’t remotely prepared to answer:

  • If an AI agent makes a hiring recommendation that produces discriminatory outcomes, who is accountable? The vendor? The company? The hiring manager who accepted the recommendation? The HR leader who selected the tool?
  • How do you performance-manage a hybrid team? If a human employee works alongside an AI agent, standard productivity metrics become meaningless. The human’s value lies in judgment, creativity, and relationship skills that aren’t captured by throughput measures.
  • What happens to team culture when half the “team” is software? The informal knowledge transfer that happens through water-cooler conversations, mentorship lunches, and hallway interactions doesn’t have an AI equivalent.

Companies building digital identities for AI agents are essentially creating a new employment category that sits outside every existing legal and organizational framework. The employment law implications alone will keep attorneys busy for the next decade.

The Regulatory Landscape Is Moving Fast

The legal environment around AI in HR is shifting faster than most companies realize.

New York City’s Local Law 144 requires employers using automated employment decision tools to conduct annual bias audits and notify candidates when AI is being used. The law has been in effect since July 2023 and enforcement actions are beginning to materialize.

Illinois’s AI Video Interview Act requires employers to notify candidates when AI analyzes video interviews and obtain consent before using AI to evaluate applicants.

Colorado’s AI Act (effective 2026) requires developers and deployers of “high-risk” AI systems — which explicitly includes employment decisions — to exercise reasonable care to avoid algorithmic discrimination. It includes notification requirements and provides a right of action for individuals harmed by AI-driven decisions.

The EU AI Act classifies AI systems used in employment, workers management, and access to self-employment as “high-risk,” subjecting them to the most stringent requirements including mandatory risk assessments, documentation, transparency, and human oversight.

For multinational companies, the compliance burden is substantial and growing. An AI screening tool that’s legal in Texas might violate regulations in New York, Colorado, or Brussels. Each jurisdiction has different audit, notification, and transparency requirements.

A Practical Playbook for the Transition

If you’re an HR leader navigating this transition, or a CEO wondering whether your HR function is evolving fast enough, here’s what the evidence suggests works:

Step 1: Audit Your HR Function by Task, Not by Role

Don’t start by asking “which roles can we eliminate?” Start by mapping every task your HR team performs and categorizing them:

  • Automatable now: Policy lookups, benefits enrollment, scheduling, basic reporting, resume screening for minimum qualifications
  • Augmentable: Performance review drafting (AI drafts, human reviews), comp analysis (AI benchmarks, human approves), training recommendations (AI suggests, human contextualizes)
  • Human-essential: Harassment investigations, termination conversations, executive coaching, organizational design, change management, bias auditing of AI tools

This task-level mapping reveals that most HR roles are bundles of automatable and human-essential work. The goal isn’t to eliminate roles wholesale but to redesign them around the human-essential work.

Step 2: Deploy AI for Service Delivery First

Start where the risk is lowest and the ROI is highest. An AI agent that answers benefits questions won’t produce bias lawsuits. It will free your HR team to do higher-value work immediately. IBM spent years on this step before expanding to more complex use cases, and the sequencing matters.

AI HR Implementation Sequence (Risk-Adjusted):
Phase 1 (Months 1-6): Service desk automation, FAQ chatbot, benefits enrollment
Phase 2 (Months 6-12): Comp benchmarking, pay equity monitoring, basic reporting
Phase 3 (Months 12-18): Performance review augmentation, L&D personalization
Phase 4 (Months 18-24): Recruiting AI (with mandatory bias auditing)
Phase 5 (Months 24+): Predictive workforce planning, org design recommendations

Step 3: Build Your Bias Auditing Capability Before You Need It

Don’t wait for a lawsuit to discover your AI screening tool has a disparate impact problem. Build — or hire — the capability to audit AI outputs for bias before deploying any tool that touches hiring, promotion, or compensation decisions.

This means:

  • Demographic impact analysis on every AI-driven decision at regular intervals
  • A/B testing where a subset of candidates goes through the traditional process alongside the AI process
  • External audit relationships with firms that specialize in algorithmic fairness
  • Documentation of every model, its training data, its decision logic, and its observed outcomes

The companies that will survive the coming wave of AI hiring lawsuits aren’t the ones with the best tools. They’re the ones with the best audit trails.

Step 4: Redesign Entry-Level Roles, Don’t Kill Them

Take the Korn Ferry warning seriously. For every entry-level position you’re tempted to replace with AI, ask: where will this team’s next manager come from?

The redesigned entry-level role looks different from the old one. Instead of data entry and report compilation, the new analyst evaluates AI outputs, flags anomalies, and learns the business through the lens of the AI tools they’ll eventually manage. It’s a more interesting job, it pays better, and it builds the pipeline you’ll need in five years.

Step 5: Invest in the Roles AI Can’t Do

The transition creates genuine opportunity to build HR capabilities that most companies have historically underinvested in:

  • Org design that’s data-driven and responsive to real-time workforce intelligence
  • Change management that helps employees adapt to working alongside AI
  • Culture architecture that maintains human connection in increasingly automated environments
  • Strategic workforce planning that models multiple scenarios and prepares for each

These aren’t administrative roles. They’re strategic functions that justify senior compensation and carry real organizational influence. The CHRO who can navigate this transition becomes the most valuable C-suite partner the CEO has.

The Bottom Line

HR is not going away. But the HR department that most companies operate today — large teams of generalists processing transactions, answering questions, and maintaining spreadsheets — is disappearing. IBM showed us the endgame. The Workday lawsuit showed us the risks. Korn Ferry showed us the second-order effects nobody planned for.

The companies that navigate this well will emerge with smaller, more senior, more strategic HR teams that deliver better outcomes for both the business and its employees. The companies that navigate it poorly will cut costs in the short term and face bias lawsuits, leadership pipeline crises, and cultural erosion in the medium term.

The window for thoughtful transition is closing. If you haven’t started mapping your HR function against AI capabilities, you’re already behind. And if you’ve started but skipped the bias auditing, you’re building on sand.

This is Part 3 of the AI & Organization Management series. Previously: AI Is Killing the Annual Performance Review. Next up: AI is reshaping how companies approach workforce planning — and the data is finally good enough to make it work.

item.title: AI Is Killing the Project Manager Role — Who Coordinates Now?
item.description:
The Status Meeting Nobody Needs Anymore

Here’s a scene playing out in thousands of companies right now: a project manager opens Monday.com, copies task statuses into a slide deck, spends 20 minutes chasing two engineers for updates, schedules a 30-minute standup, reads the same information aloud in that standup, then emails a summary to stakeholders who skim it for 45 seconds.

That ritual is dying. Not slowly. Violently.

Asana’s AI agent now auto-generates project status reports by reading task completion data, commit histories, and Slack conversations. It flags blockers before any human notices them. Monday.com’s AI does the same — plus it generates risk assessments based on historical project velocity and resource allocation patterns.
item.content:encoded:
The Status Meeting Nobody Needs Anymore

Here’s a scene playing out in thousands of companies right now: a project manager opens Monday.com, copies task statuses into a slide deck, spends 20 minutes chasing two engineers for updates, schedules a 30-minute standup, reads the same information aloud in that standup, then emails a summary to stakeholders who skim it for 45 seconds.

That ritual is dying. Not slowly. Violently.

Asana’s AI agent now auto-generates project status reports by reading task completion data, commit histories, and Slack conversations. It flags blockers before any human notices them. Monday.com’s AI does the same — plus it generates risk assessments based on historical project velocity and resource allocation patterns.

When the core deliverable of a role — “keeping everyone informed about what’s happening” — gets automated by a feature update, that role has a problem. Project managers have that problem right now.

Gartner’s widely cited prediction is specific: by 2026, 20% of organizations will use AI to flatten their organizational structure, eliminating more than half of current middle management positions. Project managers sit squarely in that blast radius. Not because their work doesn’t matter — but because the tasks that fill their calendars are exactly the kind of structured, information-routing work that AI handles better than humans.

Let’s break down what’s actually happening, what’s already gone, and what remains.

The Five PM Tasks That AI Already Does Better

1. Status Reporting and Progress Tracking

This is the most obvious one and it’s already mature. Tools like Asana Intelligence, ClickUp Brain, and Notion AI pull real-time data from tasks, code repositories, design tools, and communication channels to generate status reports automatically.

The quality isn’t “rough draft you need to polish.” It’s production-ready. ClickUp Brain generates project summaries that pull from every data source in the workspace — documents, tasks, comments, time tracking — and synthesizes them into a coherent narrative. No human needed.

A senior engineering director at a Series C fintech told me something revealing last quarter: “We realized our PMs were spending 8-10 hours a week on reporting. The AI does it in seconds, and honestly, it’s more accurate because it doesn’t forget to check the QA board.”

That’s 25% of a PM’s workweek. Gone.

2. Scheduling and Resource Allocation

Traditional project scheduling means a PM opens a Gantt chart, manually maps dependencies, estimates durations based on gut feel and past experience, then adjusts when reality diverges from the plan. This happens continuously.

Forecast.app uses machine learning trained on over 500,000 projects to predict task durations, identify resource bottlenecks, and auto-schedule work. Their system analyzes team velocity patterns, individual performance data, and project complexity to generate schedules that outperform human estimates by a meaningful margin.

Smartsheet’s AI does predictive scheduling that accounts for resource availability, skill matching, and historical completion patterns. It doesn’t just tell you the schedule — it tells you which schedule has the highest probability of on-time delivery and why.

Here’s what kills the PM’s scheduling role specifically: these tools improve over time. Every completed project feeds back into the model. A human PM’s scheduling ability improves linearly with experience. AI’s improves exponentially with data.

3. Risk Identification and Escalation

Good project managers are valued for their ability to see problems before they explode. “She has great instincts” is the highest compliment in PM culture. But those instincts are really just pattern matching — and pattern matching is exactly what machine learning does.

Wrike’s AI now provides automated risk scoring for projects based on dozens of signals: task completion velocity, resource utilization rates, dependency chain complexity, and communication patterns. When engineers stop updating tasks or Slack threads go quiet on a critical workstream, the system flags it.

Planview’s AI goes further with portfolio-level risk assessment. It can identify when multiple projects are competing for the same bottleneck resource before any individual PM would notice, because no individual PM has visibility across the full portfolio.

The uncomfortable truth: AI doesn’t have bad days. It doesn’t get distracted by politics. It doesn’t avoid flagging a risk because the executive sponsor is intimidating. For pure risk identification, AI is already more reliable than most human PMs.

4. Meeting Facilitation and Action Item Tracking

The weekly standup, the sprint retrospective, the stakeholder check-in — these meetings are PM bread and butter. Otter.ai and Fireflies.ai now attend meetings, transcribe them in real-time, extract action items, assign owners, set due dates, and follow up automatically.

Zoom’s AI Companion generates meeting summaries and automatically creates tasks in connected project management tools. Miss a meeting? The AI gives you a better summary than any human notetaker would.

But it goes beyond transcription. These tools are starting to identify decision patterns: “This topic has been discussed in three consecutive meetings without resolution. Escalation recommended.” That kind of meta-analysis — noticing that you’re going in circles — is something even experienced PMs struggle with in real-time because they’re inside the conversation.

5. Stakeholder Communication and Reporting

PMs spend enormous energy translating between audiences. Technical details for engineering, business impact for executives, timeline implications for sales. Each audience gets a different version of the same information.

AI is spectacularly good at this. Anthropic’s Claude and GPT-4 can take a technical project update and rewrite it for an executive audience in seconds. Connect that to a project management tool via API, and you have automatic multi-audience reporting.

Several teams I’ve spoken with have built exactly this: a Slack bot that monitors project channels, generates daily executive summaries, and posts them to a leadership channel. No PM involvement. No information loss. No delay.

The Amazon Signal

In October 2025, Amazon announced a major restructuring that sent shockwaves through the management consulting world. The company increased its individual contributor-to-manager ratio from 6:1 to a target of 8:1, eliminating thousands of middle management positions.

CEO Andy Jassy framed this as “reducing bureaucracy.” But the subtext was clear to anyone paying attention: AI tools had made much of the coordination layer unnecessary. When the world’s most operationally sophisticated company decides it needs fewer coordinators, the signal is unmistakable.

Amazon wasn’t alone. Meta’s Year of Efficiency in 2023 and its continuation through 2025 eliminated multiple layers of management. Google restructured its product organization multiple times, each time flattening reporting chains. Microsoft integrated Copilot so deeply into project workflows that several internal PM functions were consolidated.

The pattern across all of them: companies aren’t saying “we don’t need project management.” They’re saying “we don’t need as many humans doing project management.”

What Actually Disappears vs. What Stays

Let’s be precise about what’s happening. The PM role isn’t vanishing overnight. It’s unbundling.

Already automated (or nearly there):

  • Status report generation
  • Meeting note-taking and action item tracking
  • Schedule creation and optimization
  • Resource utilization tracking
  • Simple risk flag identification
  • Template-based stakeholder updates
  • Time tracking and capacity planning

Still requires humans (for now):

  • Navigating organizational politics
  • Building trust with skeptical stakeholders
  • Making judgment calls when data is ambiguous
  • Motivating teams through difficult phases
  • Negotiating scope changes with product leadership
  • Managing vendor relationships with complex contracts
  • Crisis communication and damage control

The awkward middle ground:

  • Dependency management (AI handles simple chains; humans needed for cross-team political dependencies)
  • Retrospectives (AI can identify patterns, but humans still need to facilitate the emotional processing)
  • Sprint planning (AI can optimize, but humans still need to negotiate commitments)

The uncomfortable math: the “already automated” list represents roughly 50-60% of how most PMs spend their time. The “still requires humans” list represents the parts of the job most PMs were never formally trained for.

The New Shape of Project Coordination

So who coordinates when the PM layer thins out? Three models are emerging.

Model 1: The AI-Augmented PM (30% of Current Headcount)

Some companies keep PMs but dramatically reduce headcount. One PM who previously managed two teams now manages six, because AI handles all the mechanical coordination work. The remaining PMs are essentially relationship managers and organizational navigators who happen to use AI tools constantly.

This is what Wrike and Forecast advocate: AI as amplifier, not replacement. The PM becomes a “strategic orchestrator” rather than a “status tracker.”

The catch: this model requires PMs who are genuinely skilled at the human elements — politics, motivation, stakeholder management. Many PMs built careers primarily on organizational skills and process rigor. Those PMs struggle in this model because their core competency just got automated.

Model 2: The Embedded Technical Lead

In this model, the PM role dissolves entirely. Technical leads absorb the coordination responsibilities, aided by AI tools that make the admin burden manageable for someone whose primary job is engineering.

Spotify’s squad model always tilted this direction, with engineering managers handling both technical and project coordination. AI tools make this viable at larger scales. When scheduling, reporting, and risk tracking are automated, the remaining coordination work — unblocking dependencies, negotiating priorities — is close enough to an engineering manager’s natural workflow that a dedicated PM adds more overhead than value.

Several YC-backed startups in the 2024-2025 cohorts launched with this model from day one. No dedicated PM role. Technical leads coordinate using AI tools. They report that project health is comparable to companies with traditional PM functions, at significantly lower organizational complexity.

Model 3: The AI Agent as PM

This is the bleeding edge, and it’s where my own work intersects. I run multiple AI agents coordinated through OpenClaw and Telegram. One agent handles quantitative research, another manages a trading portfolio, a third coordinates between them. The “project management” — scheduling tasks, tracking progress, generating reports, flagging risks — is entirely handled by AI agents.

The daily standup is a scheduled cron job. The status report is auto-generated from agent memory files. Risk identification happens through automated monitoring. The agents even run retrospectives by analyzing their own logs for patterns.

Is this ready for a 500-person engineering organization? No. But for teams under 50 running well-defined processes, it works surprisingly well. The coordination overhead drops to nearly zero because there’s no human in the loop for routine management tasks.

The Skills Gap Nobody’s Talking About

Here’s what makes this transition particularly painful: the project management profession spent two decades professionalizing around the wrong skills for an AI-augmented world.

The PMP certification, held by over 1.4 million professionals worldwide, tests knowledge of process frameworks, scheduling methodologies, and risk management techniques. These are precisely the skills AI automates most effectively.

Meanwhile, the skills that remain valuable — organizational influence, conflict resolution, stakeholder psychology, crisis leadership — have no standardized certification. The PM profession invested its credibility in demonstrating mastery of processes, and AI just commoditized processes.

PMI seems to recognize this. Their 2024 Global Survey found that 82% of senior leaders expect AI to significantly impact project management practices. PMI launched AI-focused credentials and training modules. But they’re playing catch-up with a transformation already underway.

The project managers who will thrive are the ones who were always a bit uncomfortable being “just” PMs — the ones who spent time building genuine relationships, developing business domain expertise, and learning to navigate organizational power dynamics. These PMs treated project management as a platform for organizational impact, not as an end in itself.

The PMs in trouble are the ones whose primary value proposition is “I keep the trains running on time.” AI keeps trains running on time now. Better.

What This Means for Organizations

If you’re a CTO, VP of Engineering, or COO reading this, here’s the practical framework:

Phase 1 (Now - Q2 2026): Audit PM Time Allocation

Have your PMs track their time for two weeks across these categories:

  • Mechanical coordination (status, scheduling, tracking)
  • Information routing (translating between audiences)
  • Relationship management (stakeholder alignment, conflict resolution)
  • Strategic decision support (scope negotiation, trade-off analysis)

If more than 40% falls in the first two categories, you have automation opportunity.

Phase 2 (Q3-Q4 2026): Pilot AI-First Coordination

Pick two or three projects and run them with AI-powered coordination and either no dedicated PM or a PM explicitly redeployed to purely strategic work. Measure the outcomes honestly: delivery speed, team satisfaction, stakeholder satisfaction, defect rates.

Phase 3 (2027): Restructure

Based on pilot results, redesign your PM organization. Most companies will land on 40-60% fewer dedicated PMs, with remaining PMs covering more scope per person and spending their time exclusively on high-judgment work.

What to watch out for: The transition period is messy. Teams accustomed to PM-driven coordination will initially struggle with self-coordination, even with AI tools. Budget 2-3 months for adjustment. The teams that adapt fastest are usually the ones with the strongest engineering leadership — another reason the “embedded tech lead” model is gaining traction.

The Prompt That Does a PM’s Weekly Report

This isn’t theoretical. Here’s a production prompt framework for generating PM-quality weekly status reports using AI:

You are a project status analyst. Given the following data sources:
- Task board export (JSON from Jira/Asana/Linear)
- Git commit log (last 7 days)
- Slack channel summary (key threads from #project-{name})

Generate a weekly status report with these sections:
1. Executive Summary (3 sentences max)
2. Milestone Progress (table: milestone | status | delta from plan)
3. Key Risks (ranked by impact × probability, with mitigation status)
4. Blockers Requiring Escalation (only items that need leadership action)
5. Next Week Focus Areas (top 3 priorities with owners)

Rules:
- No filler language. Every sentence must contain information.
- Flag any milestone where actual velocity < 80% of planned velocity.
- If a risk was flagged last week and mitigation hasn't started, mark it RED.
- Keep total length under 400 words.

Connect this to a weekly cron job that pulls data from your project management tool’s API, and you have automated status reporting that’s honestly better than what most PMs produce manually. The AI doesn’t forget to check edge cases, doesn’t accidentally omit the test environment blocker, and doesn’t soften bad news to avoid uncomfortable conversations.

The Career Pivot for Current PMs

If you’re a project manager reading this with a growing sense of dread, here’s the honest advice:

Move toward product management. Product managers make decisions about what to build and why. That requires customer empathy, market understanding, and strategic thinking — all things AI assists but can’t replace. Many PMs already work closely enough with product that the transition is natural.

Develop deep domain expertise. A PM who deeply understands healthcare compliance, or financial regulation, or manufacturing operations brings judgment that generic AI cannot. Domain expertise makes you the person AI tools consult, not the person AI tools replace.

Learn to manage AI agents. This sounds self-serving coming from someone who runs AI agent infrastructure, but it’s genuine: the ability to configure, monitor, and orchestrate AI agents is becoming a distinct and valuable skill. Someone needs to decide which processes to automate, design the agent workflows, and handle the edge cases. That “someone” looks a lot like a PM who understands both process design and technology.

Build your political capital. The parts of PM work that survive are the deeply human parts: navigating competing priorities, building coalitions, managing up. If you’ve been treating these as secondary to your Gantt chart skills, reverse that priority immediately.

Stop identifying as a “project manager.” Start identifying as a “business operations leader” or “delivery strategist” or whatever title captures the high-judgment work you do. The label matters because it shapes how others perceive your value — and how you perceive it yourself.

Where This Goes

The endpoint isn’t “no project management.” It’s “project management without project managers” — the function persists but gets distributed across AI tools, technical leads, and a much smaller number of senior strategic coordinators.

Within three years, I expect the typical software company to have 60-70% fewer dedicated PMs than it does today. The coordination work doesn’t disappear — it gets absorbed by AI and redistributed to the people closest to the actual work.

For the profession, this is an existential reckoning. For organizations, it’s an opportunity to reduce overhead and improve execution speed simultaneously. For individual PMs willing to adapt, it’s a chance to shed the parts of the job they probably didn’t love anyway and focus on work that actually requires human judgment.

The status meeting nobody needs anymore? That’s just the beginning.

This is Part 4 of the AI & Organization Management series. Previously: AI Is Gutting HR Departments — Here’s What Actually Replaces Them. Next up: how AI is reshaping corporate training and L&D departments.

item.title: AI Is Killing the Corporate L&D Department
item.description:
The corporate Learning & Development department has operated on the same basic model for decades: a team of instructional designers builds courses, an LMS hosts them, trainers deliver them, and everyone fills out a satisfaction survey pretending they learned something. The whole operation runs on a cycle measured in months—needs analysis, design, development, pilot, rollout.

That cycle is now measured in minutes.

AI agents can analyze a role’s skill gaps, generate a personalized curriculum, build interactive content, quiz the learner adaptively, and measure actual performance improvement—all without a single instructional designer touching the project. The $400 billion corporate training industry is staring at the same structural collapse that hit travel agents, stockbrokers, and newspaper classified departments before it.
item.content:encoded:
The corporate Learning & Development department has operated on the same basic model for decades: a team of instructional designers builds courses, an LMS hosts them, trainers deliver them, and everyone fills out a satisfaction survey pretending they learned something. The whole operation runs on a cycle measured in months—needs analysis, design, development, pilot, rollout.

That cycle is now measured in minutes.

AI agents can analyze a role’s skill gaps, generate a personalized curriculum, build interactive content, quiz the learner adaptively, and measure actual performance improvement—all without a single instructional designer touching the project. The $400 billion corporate training industry is staring at the same structural collapse that hit travel agents, stockbrokers, and newspaper classified departments before it.

This isn’t a prediction. It’s already happening.

The L&D Department Was Already Broken

Before we talk about AI replacing L&D, we need to be honest: most corporate training was already failing spectacularly.

The numbers are damning. According to Training Orchestra’s 2026 industry analysis, U.S. organizations spent $101.8 billion on training between 2024 and 2025. Yet research consistently shows that employees forget 70% of training content within 24 hours and 90% within a week—the famous Ebbinghaus forgetting curve that L&D departments have been aware of for over a century and still haven’t solved.

The fundamental problem is structural. Traditional L&D operates on a broadcast model: design one course, push it to everyone, check the compliance box. The team that built a three-day onboarding program in 2019 is still running essentially the same program in 2026, maybe with updated screenshots. The instructional designer who spent six weeks building an e-learning module on data privacy has no mechanism to adapt that content to the 47 different roles that are forced to take it.

Meanwhile, the actual skills employees need are changing faster than any L&D department can catalog them. The World Economic Forum estimates that 40% of workers will need reskilling due to AI and automation over the next three years. That’s not a training challenge L&D departments at their current scale and speed can solve.

They were already failing. AI just made the failure impossible to ignore.

What AI Actually Does Better

The case for AI replacing traditional L&D functions isn’t theoretical. It maps to specific, concrete capabilities that are already deployed in production environments.

Personalized Skill Gap Analysis
